name: nerdynovelistai-stage3-worldbuilding
label: NerdyNovelistAI Stage 3 — Dossier to Worldbuilding
description: Builds a complete World Bible from a story dossier, ported from the n8n "StoryHackerAI - Stage 3 - Dossier to Worldbuilding" workflow (Mercury). Expands every worldbuilding element the dossier names, using a 12-category expansion rubric (High-level, Settings, Objects, Magic/Tech, Groups/Races, Gods/Deities, Geography, Population/Politics, Culture, History/Lore, Religion, Languages), then runs a rigorous consistency/plausibility logic check across the whole world and applies the fixes. The source looped per worldbuilding-element via splitInBatches (parse element list -> expand each by category -> logic-check -> rewrite); BookClaw has no generic per-item loop, so this is FLATTENED into three single passes that build the full bible in one go. Model overrides mirror the source nodes (Expand on anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6, Logic Check on google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview, Rewrite on google/gemini-3-flash-preview, all via OpenRouter) and are PROVISIONAL — adjust to taste. Inputs the source read from files (dossier, tropes/themes/worldbuilding templates) are re-expressed as "the dossier in your context above" and "the book's genre guide".

steps.label: World Bible
steps.promptTemplate:
This is a complex task. You are not allowed to perform at a mediocre level. You are an expert developmental editor, worldbuilder, and storyteller.

Write the complete WORLD BIBLE for "{{title}}". Build it from the dossier in your context above (the story's worldbuilding, character, and plot details), the author's concept ({{description}}), and the genre conventions, tropes, and themes in the book's genre guide in your context.

First, identify EVERY distinct worldbuilding element the dossier names — do not invent new ones and do not omit any. Then write a fleshed-out, self-contained profile for each element. For each element, first determine which category it belongs to (not every story uses every category; some use only one or two), then expand it using that category's rubric below.

CATEGORIES & EXPANSION RUBRICS:

High-level Worldbuilding — a comprehensive overview of the world's foundational rules and identity (the "feel and logic" north star, not specific places or systems). Header `# High-level Worldbuilding Element: [NAME]`; one italicized evocative tagline beneath it; `##` subheaders as relevant for Core Premise, What Makes This Element Distinct, Internal Logic & Rules, History; 1-2 sentences of prose per section, no bullets.

Setting(s)/Locations — a lived-in, three-dimensional place, not a stage backdrop. Header `# Setting/Location: [NAME]`; italicized tagline; `##` subheaders for Physical Description and Layout, Atmosphere & Emotional Tone, Who Lives Here / Who Passes Through, Purpose & Function in the World, Purpose & Function in the Story, Secrets & Hidden Layers, History & How It Has Changed, Connections to Other Locations; prose (1-2 sentences) per section, except Secrets & Hidden Layers which may use a short bulleted list.

Objects/Artifacts — an object with weight, history, and consequence, fitting the world that made it. Header `# Object/Artifact: [NAME]`; italicized tagline capturing its essence or reputation; `##` subheaders as relevant for Physical Description, Origins & Who Made It, Purpose & How It Works, Costs & Limitations, History & Chain of Ownership, Current Location & State, How It Is Perceived by the World, Myths & Legends Surrounding It; prose (1-2 sentences) per section, except Costs & Limitations as a short bulleted list; end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on how it generates conflict, obsession, moral weight, or plot momentum, and who wants it, fears it, or wants it destroyed.

Magic Systems/Technology — mechanics, limits, and costs specific enough to use consistently; it should create story problems, not just solve them. Header `# Magic System/Technology: [NAME]`; italicized one-sentence summary; `##` subheaders for How It Works, Who Can Access It & Why, Costs & Limitations, Hard Rules (always true), Soft Rules (usually true, can bend), Social Perception & Power Dynamics, History & Evolution, What Happens When It's Misused; Hard Rules and Soft Rules as bulleted lists, all else prose (1-2 sentences); end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on the conflict, stakes, or character tension it generates.

Groups/Races — a real community with internal diversity, fractures, and complexity, not a monolith. Header `# Group/Race: [NAME]`; italicized tagline (how they see themselves or how the world sees them); `##` subheaders for Origins, Defining Characteristics, Internal Hierarchy & Social Structure, Core Values & Taboos, Relationships with Other Groups, Greatest Achievements & Deepest Traumas, How They See Themselves vs. How Others See Them, Diversity Within the Group; prose (1-2 sentences) per section, except Relationships with Other Groups as a short bulleted list (one line per group); end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on conflicts, tensions, or story opportunities.

Gods/Deities — an ambiguous divine entity whose nature reveals as much about mortals as divinity. Header `# God/Deity: [NAME]`; italicized epithet or prayer fragment; `##` subheaders for Domain & Areas of Influence, Origin & Mythology, Personality & Motivations, How They Interact with the Mortal World, What They Want / What They Offer, How Different Groups Interpret Them, Contradictions & Mysteries; prose (1-2 sentences) per section; end with a `## The Truth Behind the Myth` section noting (for the author's eyes only) what is actually true versus what mortals believe, even if that truth is "unknown".

Geography/Nature — a natural force with agency, not scenery. Header `# Geography/Nature: [NAME]`; italicized tagline (mood or presence); `##` subheaders for Physical Description & Scale, Climate & Seasonal Patterns, Flora & Fauna, Resources & Dangers, Effect on Cultures & Civilizations Nearby, Spiritual or Magical Significance, How It Is Changing Over Time; prose (1-2 sentences) per section, except Flora & Fauna and Resources & Dangers which may use short bulleted lists; end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on how it drives plot, creates conflict, or shapes character.

Population/Politics — power structures and political pressures that force hard choices. Header `# Population/Politics: [NAME]`; italicized tagline (the central political tension); `##` subheaders for Who Holds Power & How They Got It, Who Is Excluded & Why, Major Factions & Their Agendas, Formal Laws vs. Unspoken Rules, Fault Lines & Potential Flashpoints, How Ordinary People Experience Political Life, Corruption / Reform & Resistance; prose (1-2 sentences) per section, except Major Factions & Their Agendas as a bulleted list (one faction per bullet with a brief description); end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on the story pressure it creates.

Culture — the day-to-day texture of life characters swim in unconsciously, not exotic customs bolted on. Header `# Culture: [NAME]`; italicized tagline (what defines this culture's spirit); `##` subheaders for Art / Music & Storytelling, Food / Fashion & Material Culture, Core Values & Shame Triggers, Rituals & Life Milestones, How Class / Gender & Identity Shape Cultural Experience, Subcultures & Counter-Movements, Cultural Influence On & From Others; prose (1-2 sentences) per section; end with a `## Details to Use in Prose` section — 5-8 specific, sensory, concrete details a writer could drop into a scene without stopping to explain.

History/Lore — a history that still breathes in the present, a living wound or source of pride. Header `# History/Lore: [NAME]`; italicized tagline (why it still matters); `##` subheaders for What Actually Happened, The Major Players & Their Motivations, The Official Version, What Has Been Lost or Suppressed, Myths & Legends That Grew from This History, How This History Lives in the Present; prose (1-2 sentences) per section; end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on how it can be weaponized, mourned, or rediscovered.

Religion/Beliefs — a living, complicated institution that gives meaning while wielding real social power. Header `# Religion/Beliefs: [NAME]`; italicized core creed, prayer, or saying; `##` subheaders for Core Theology & Cosmology, Rituals & Sacred Practices, Clergy / Leadership & Institutional Power, Intersection with Politics & Social Life, Orthodoxy vs. Heterodoxy, How It Handles Doubt & Heresy, How Non-Believers & Rival Faiths View It, What It Gives & What It Costs; prose (1-2 sentences) per section; end with a `## Narrative Implications` section of 3-5 bullets on the conflict, crisis of faith, or moral complexity it generates.

Languages — a language profiled by social/cultural/emotional function, not grammar; what speaking or not speaking it means. Header `# Language: [NAME]`; italicized tagline (what it sounds or feels like to hear); `##` subheaders for Origins & Linguistic Family, What Speaking It Signals, Key Untranslatable Words or Phrases, How It Has Changed Over Time, Dialects & Registers, Language as Power or Resistance; prose (1-2 sentences) per section, except Key Untranslatable Words or Phrases as a bulleted list (word/phrase in bold then its explanation, 3-5 entries); end with a `## Details to Use in Prose` section — 4-6 specific ways a writer could use this language in a scene to convey character, class, or cultural tension without stopping to explain.

Keep every section to 1-2 sentences unless a rubric states otherwise. Honor any author notes and stay strictly consistent with the dossier, the genre guide, and elements you've already written within this same document. Output ONLY the assembled World Bible (every element profiled in turn) — no preamble or commentary. This is a production artifact.

steps.label: Worldbuilding Logic Check
steps.promptTemplate:
This is a complex task. You are not allowed to perform at a mediocre level. You are performing a rigorous LOGIC CHECK on the World Bible generated above. Your job is to ensure it is 100% consistent, plausible, and logical, and consistent with the story dossier, the author's concept, and the book's genre guide. You are diagnosing and producing an improvement plan here, not rewriting the bible.

Go through EVERY worldbuilding element in the World Bible above and flag any issues against this checklist:

1. Dossier Consistency — Does every element align with the worldbuilding, character, and plot details in the dossier in your context? No contradictions with story events, character roles, or other worldbuilding elements?

2. Internal Cross-Consistency — Is each element aligned with the OTHER elements already fleshed out in the same World Bible? Flag any element that contradicts another.

3. Author Notes & Concept Alignment — Does each element honor the author's concept ({{description}}) and any specific author instructions?

4. Genre & Template Fit — Does each element feel appropriate for the genre and fit the role it plays in the world, per the book's genre guide in your context?

5. Internal Logic & Consistency — Do this element's rules hold up under scrutiny? Any logical contradictions within the element itself? If it has limits or costs, are they applied consistently, or do they bend conveniently for plot reasons?

6. Plausibility & Cause/Effect — Do the social, political, economic, or physical consequences make sense (e.g. a deadly plague should affect labor, trade, and religion, not just one character)? Does history flow logically from cause to effect, or do events happen only because the plot needs them? Are power structures realistic given the resources, geography, and technology available?

7. Story Integration — Does this element create meaningful pressure, stakes, or choices for the characters, or is it just set dressing? Is it developed appropriately for its role? Does it connect to at least one other worldbuilding element so the world feels interconnected rather than modular?

8. Thematic Resonance — Does this element reflect or reinforce the story's central themes? Could it serve as a metaphor or lens for the story's core questions? Does it feel intentional, or added for color without deeper purpose?

OUTPUT: For each flagged element, list the issues and write a concrete improvement plan describing how to fix it while keeping it consistent with the story and genre. Output the critique and improvement plan only — do not rewrite the World Bible.

steps.label: World Bible Revision
steps.promptTemplate: Apply the IMPROVEMENT PLAN from the logic check above to the World Bible for "{{title}}" (also in your context above). Implement ONLY the suggested changes; do not change anything else about the World Bible. Reproduce the ENTIRE World Bible with the same formatting and structure as the original, but with the flagged fixes made — every element, every header, every section preserved unless the plan specifically calls for a change. Output only the complete, revised World Bible (this is the production artifact).
